---
title: 'Midterm'
author: 'Sabah Khan'
date: "Due: March 8th, 2026"
output:
  html_document:           # output document format
    toc: yes               # add table contents
    toc_float: yes         # toc_property: floating
    toc_depth: 4           # depth of TOC headings
    fig_width: 6           # global figure width
    fig_height: 4          # global figure height
    fig_caption: yes       # add figure caption
    number_sections: yes   # numbering section headings
    toc_collapsed: yes     # TOC subheading clapsing
    code_folding: hide     # folding/showing code 
    code_download: yes     # allow to download complete RMarkdown source code
    smooth_scroll: yes     # scrolling text of the document
    theme: lumen           # visual theme for HTML document only
    highlight: tango       # code syntax hightlighting styles
  pdf_document: 
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: 4
    fig_caption: yes
    number_sections: yes
  word_document:
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: '4'
---

```{css, echo = FALSE}
div#TOC li {     /* table of content  */
    list-style:upper-roman;
    background-image:none;
    background-repeat:none;
    background-position:0;
}

h1.title {    /* level 1 header of title  */
  font-size: 24px;
  font-weight: bold;
  color: DarkRed;
  text-align: center;
}

h4.author { /* Header 4 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
  font-size: 18px;
  font-weight: bold;
  font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
  color: DarkRed;
  text-align: center;
}

h4.date { /* Header 4 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
  font-size: 18px;
  font-weight: bold;
  font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
  color: DarkBlue;
  text-align: center;
}

h1 { /* Header 1 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
    font-size: 20px;
    font-weight: bold;
    font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
    color: darkred;
    text-align: center;
}

h2 { /* Header 2 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
    font-size: 18px;
    font-weight: bold;
    font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
    color: navy;
    text-align: left;
}

h3 { /* Header 3 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
    font-size: 16px;
    font-weight: bold;
    font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
    color: navy;
    text-align: left;
}

h4 { /* Header 4 - and the author and data headers use this too  */
    font-size: 14px;
  font-weight: bold;
    font-family: "Times New Roman", Times, serif;
    color: darkred;
    text-align: left;
}

/* Add dots after numbered headers */
.header-section-number::after {
  content: ".";
}
```

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# code chunk specifies whether the R code, warnings, and output 
# will be included in the output files.

if (!require("knitr")) {                      # use conditional statement to detect
   install.packages("knitr")                  # whether a package was installed in
   library(knitr)     
if (!require("imager"))
  installed.packages("imager")
  library(imager)
if (!require("latex2exp"))
  installed.packages("latex2exp")
  library(latex2exp)
if (!require("MASS"))
  installed.packages("MASS")
  library(MASS)

  
                                                # your machine. If not, install it and
}                                             # load it to the working directory.
#
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE,            # include code chunk in the output file
                      warning = FALSE,        # sometimes, you code may produce warning messages,
                                              # you can choose to include the warning messages in
                                              # the output file. 
                      results = TRUE,         # you can also decide whether to include the output
                                              # in the output file.
                      message = FALSE,        # suppress messages 
                      comment = NA            # remove the default leading hash tags in the output
                      )   
```


# Question 1


```{r, fig.align='center', out.width="60%"}
knitr::include_graphics("C://Users//Sabah//OneDrive//Documents//Mathematical Statistics II - Peng/midterm1.jpg")
```

```{r}

x<-c (17.3, 24.8, 8.2, 31.5, 14.1, 42.7, 11.9, 55.3, 21.4, 9.7, 36.2, 18.6, 63.1, 13.2, 28.9, 
47.5, 19.8, 7.5, 33.4, 52.1, 16.0, 25.9, 38.7, 10.3, 44.2, 22.5, 58.6, 12.8, 30.3, 48.9, 
20.1, 35.4, 15.7, 60.2, 26.7, 41.3, 18.1, 53.8, 23.9, 46.2, 29.4, 37.6, 14.9, 50.5, 32.8, 
19.3, 56.7, 11.2, 39.5, 27.1)


## Finding the first and 2nd moments
m1 <- mean(x)
m2 <- mean(x^2)

mom_sigma_sq <- log(m2) - 2*log(m1)
mom_mu <- log(m1)-0.5*mom_sigma_sq
mom_mu_LN <- exp(mom_mu + 0.5*mom_sigma_sq)

cat(
  "  Sample Moments: m1 = ", m1,
  ", m2 = ", m2,
  "\n  MoM estimates: mu_hat = ", round(mom_mu, 3),
  ", sigma_sq_hat = ", round(mom_sigma_sq, 3),
  ", mu_LN_hat = ", round(mom_mu_LN, 3),
  "\n"
)

```

# Question 2

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width="60%"}
knitr::include_graphics("C://Users//Sabah//OneDrive//Documents//Mathematical Statistics II - Peng/midterm2.jpg")
```

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width="60%"}
knitr::include_graphics("C://Users//Sabah//OneDrive//Documents//Mathematical Statistics II - Peng/midterm3.jpg")
```

# Question 3

Part 1

```{r}

# Log-Normal log-likelihood function
lognormal.loglik <- function(params, data) {
  mu <- params[1]  # passing the parameters
  sigma_sq <- params[2]
  
  
  ##
  n <- length(data)   # sample size
  
  # Log-likelihood for Log-Normal distribution
  loglik <- - (n/2) * log(2*pi) - (n/2) * log(sigma_sq) - (1/(2*sigma_sq)) * sum((log(data) - mu)^2)
  
  return(-loglik)    # Return negative for minimization
}
  
## Score (gradient) equation

lognormal.score <- function(params, data) {
  mu <- params[1]
  sigma_sq <- params[2]
  
  n <- length(data)
  
  # Gradient for mu
  grad_mu <- (1/sigma_sq) * sum(log(data) - mu) #score function for mu

# Gradient for sigma_squared
  grad_sigma_sq <- -(n/(2*sigma_sq)) + (1/(2*sigma_sq^2)) * sum((log(data) - mu)^2) #score function for sigma_sq
  
 
  return(c(grad_mu, grad_sigma_sq))
}


# Perform optimization
initial.params <- start <- c(mu = mean(log(x)), sigma_sq = var(log(x))) #starting points from sample data

# Using optim with Nelder-Mead method
mle.result <- optim(
  par = initial.params,
  fn = lognormal.loglik, #removed gradient because I was getting an error message for "ERROR: ABNORMAL_TERMINATION_IN_LNSRCH"
         data = x,
         method = "L-BFGS-B", #these next two lines were added with the help of AI. I could not figure out why my mu_LN wouldn't run as a third parameter inside optim and kept getting error codes.  
  lower   = c(-Inf, 1e-6), 
  hessian = TRUE,
  

)

mu_mle       <- mle.result$par["mu"] #These lines were added with the help of AI to determine the mu_LN_hat, which I was struggling with. These lines extract the MLE's
sigma_sq_mle <- mle.result$par["sigma_sq"]

mu_LN_mle <- exp(mu_mle + 0.5 * sigma_sq_mle) # This line computes the MLE of mu-LN

# Output results
cat(
  "MLE Estimates:\n",
  "  mu_hat       =", round(mu_mle, 3),       "\n",
  "  sigma_sq_hat =", round(sigma_sq_mle, 3), "\n",
  "  mu_LN_hat    =", round(mu_LN_mle, 3),    "\n"
)
##
mle.result
  
```  

```{r}
get_mu_LN_mle <- function(data) {
  
  initial.params<-c(mu = mean(log(data)), sigma_sq = var(log(data))) #defining the initial parameters for mu_LN_MLE based on the closed form expressions for the score functions

mu_LN_MLE_result <- optim(
  par = initial.params,
  fn = lognormal.loglik,
  gr = lognormal.score,
  data = data,
  method = "L-BFGS-B",
  lower   = c(-Inf, 1e-6), 
  hessian = FALSE
  
)
  
  mu_mle       <- mu_LN_MLE_result$par["mu"]
sigma_sq_mle <- mu_LN_MLE_result$par["sigma_sq"]



mu_LN_MLE <- exp(mu_mle + 0.5*sigma_sq_mle)
return(mu_LN_MLE)
  
}





```  


```{r}
### Bootstrap sampling begins 

bt.sample.vec = NULL      # define an empty vector to hold MLE's of mu_LN's of repeated samples.
for(i in 1:1000){              # starting for-loop to take bootstrap samples with n = 50
  ith.bt.sample = sample(x = x, # Original sample with 50 recovery times
                       size = 50,             # sample size = 50 is equal to the sample size
                    replace = TRUE            # MUST use WITH REPLACEMENT!!
                       )                      # this is the i-th Bootstrap sample
  bt.sample.vec[i] = get_mu_LN_mle(ith.bt.sample) # calculate the mu_LN of i-th bootstrap sample and 
                                              # save it in the empty vector: bt.sample.vec
}



```  

```{r}
###
hist(bt.sample.vec,                     # data used for histogram
     breaks = 20,                            # specify number of vertical bars
     probability = TRUE,
       xlab = "Bootstrap sample mu_LN_hats",      # change the label of x-axis
      # add a title to the histogram
        main="Bootstrap Sampling Distribution mu_LN_hats",
          cex.main = 0.9,
       col.main = "navy")   
lines(density(bt.sample.vec), col = "skyblue", lwd = 2)

``` 

```{r}
###
hist(bt.sample.vec,                     # data used for histogram
     breaks = 20,                            # specify number of vertical bars
     probability = TRUE,
       xlab = "Bootstrap sample mu_LN_hats",      # change the label of x-axis
      # add a title to the histogram
        main="Bootstrap Sampling Distribution mu_LN_hats",
          cex.main = 0.9,
       col.main = "navy")   
lines(density(bt.sample.vec), col = "skyblue", lwd = 2)

#MLE estimates from output
mu_hat   <- 3.269 
   sigma_sq_hat <- 0.327 
   mu_LN_hat   <- 30.943

   n <-length(x)
   
   #defining asymptotic distribution for mu_LN
asym <- sqrt(sigma_sq_hat)/sqrt(n)

# Create density plot 
x_range <- seq(0,50, length.out = 1000)
asym_curve <- dnorm(x_range, mean = mu_LN_hat, sd = asym)

lines(x_range, asym_curve, col ="red", lwd = 2, lty=2 )
   
``` 


The sampling distribution of the sample mean of the lognormal distribution using maximum likelihood and bootstrapping appears to be much wider than the asymptotic curve. This makes sense to me intuitively. The MLE bootstrapping histogram is based on sample or empirical data and will have more variability than the asymptoptic curve, which is based on a large N and uses the central limit theorem. It is a theoretical curve based on a large sample, so I expect it to be narrower than the empirical curve. For a finite sample, it is probably better to use empirical data to construct a curve than a theoretical example based on an increasing N. 


Extra Credit not attempted - I have not gone over the subject material.
